expanded_description: The quadrilaterals studied in this unit form a logical hierarchy based on their properties. Each type inherits all properties of the categories above it and adds its own distinguishing features. Understanding this family tree provides a powerful organizational framework for geometric reasoning. This review section consolidates the entire unit by mapping the complete hierarchy and summarizing the defining properties at each level.

core_concept.principle: Quadrilaterals are organized in a nested hierarchy. At the top is the general quadrilateral. Adding one pair of parallel sides yields a trapezoid; adding two pairs yields a parallelogram. A parallelogram with four right angles is a rectangle. A parallelogram with four congruent sides is a rhombus. A quadrilateral that is both a rectangle and a rhombus is a square. An isosceles trapezoid is a trapezoid with congruent legs. A kite stands outside the parallel-based hierarchy. Each level inherits all properties of its ancestors.
core_concept.explanation: Think of the quadrilateral family as a tree. The trunk is 'Quadrilateral.' Two main branches split off: one for parallel sides (trapezoids and parallelograms) and one for kites. The parallel branch splits again: exactly one pair gives trapezoids, two pairs give parallelograms. The parallelogram branch flowers into rectangles (add right angles), rhombuses (add equal sides), and at the intersection, squares (both). Every property flows downward—a square has every property of a rhombus, rectangle, and parallelogram. This hierarchy means you only need to prove the most specific classification; all the more general properties come for free.

image.alt: Tree diagram: Quadrilateral root. Branches: Kite and Trapezoid. From Trapezoid: Isosceles Trapezoid and Parallelogram. From Parallelogram: Rectangle and Rhombus. Intersection: Square. Each node lists defining properties.
image.caption: The Quadrilateral Family Tree: properties flow downward through the hierarchy.

sections.introduction: The quadrilateral family tree organizes seven types into a logical inheritance structure. Understanding where each type sits and what it inherits is the key to efficient geometric reasoning.

introduction_image.alt: Full family tree with all seven types, arrows showing inheritance. Each type labeled with defining condition.
introduction_image.caption: Quadrilateral hierarchy: from general to specific, properties cascade downward.

key_concept.definition: The hierarchy (most general to most specific): Quadrilateral → Trapezoid (at least one pair parallel sides) → Parallelogram (both pairs parallel) → Rectangle (parallelogram + four right angles), Rhombus (parallelogram + four congruent sides) → Square (both rectangle and rhombus). Separate branches: Isosceles Trapezoid (trapezoid + congruent legs). Kite (two pairs adjacent congruent sides). A square is also a rhombus, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, and quadrilateral. Properties cascade downward through the tree.
key_concept.context: This hierarchy is a logical tool. If you prove a quadrilateral is a rhombus, you automatically know all parallelogram and quadrilateral properties. If you then prove it has a right angle, it becomes a square, inheriting rectangle properties as well. No need to re-prove inherited properties.

image.alt: Complete family tree with property lists at each node. Sidebar showing inheritance relationships.
image.caption: Complete hierarchy with inheritance rules.

process_flow.process: Locate the Quadrilateral in the Hierarchy
process_flow.description: Identify the most specific classification using definitions and tests from the unit.
process_flow.mechanism: Start at Quadrilateral. One pair parallel? → Trapezoid. Two pairs? → Parallelogram. Four right angles? → Rectangle. Four congruent sides? → Rhombus. Both? → Square. No parallel sides but two pairs adjacent equal? → Kite.
applications: Four right angles + four equal sides → Square.
Four equal sides, no right angles → Rhombus.

image.alt: Flowchart from Quadrilateral through decision points to each classification.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the most specific classification.

process_flow.description: Every property at that level and all levels above applies.
process_flow.mechanism: Square inherits all from rhombus, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, quadrilateral. Rhombus inherits from parallelogram, trapezoid, quadrilateral. Rectangle inherits from parallelogram, trapezoid, quadrilateral. Kite has only quadrilateral properties plus its own.
process_flow.result: Complete property set for the quadrilateral.
applications: A square has perpendicular diagonals (from rhombus), congruent diagonals (from rectangle), bisecting diagonals (from parallelogram). All automatic.

image.alt: Square with long property list, each tagged with source ancestor.
image.caption: Step 2: Read downward—all ancestor properties apply.

classifications.value: A comprehensive table showing which properties each type possesses.
characteristics: Columns: opposite sides parallel, opposite sides ≅, opposite angles ≅, consecutive supplementary, diagonals bisect, diagonals ≅, diagonals ⟂, diagonals bisect angles, all sides ≅, all angles 90°.
Checkmarks show which properties each type has.
The pattern of checkmarks visually demonstrates the hierarchy.

examples.explanation: A square has checkmarks in all ten columns. A rhombus has all except the rectangle-specific ones (diagonals ≅, all angles 90°). A rectangle has all except the rhombus-specific ones (diagonals ⟂, diagonals bisect angles, all sides ≅).

image.alt: Table with quadrilateral types as rows and properties as columns. Checkmarks showing inheritance pattern.
image.caption: Property inheritance table: a visual representation of the hierarchy.

image.alt: Complete hierarchy with tree, property table, and Venn diagram.
image.caption: Quadrilateral Family Tree overview.

sections.introduction: Apply the hierarchy to classify quadrilaterals, determine inherited properties, and understand set relationships.

worked_examples.title: Hierarchy Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Four congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals, diagonals not congruent. Classify.
2) Both pairs opposite sides parallel, one right angle, all sides equal. Classify.
3) List properties an isosceles trapezoid inherits.
4) True/False: All rectangles are squares.
5) A quadrilateral is both a kite and a trapezoid. What is it?

step_by_step.explanation: Four congruent sides → rhombus. Perpendicular diagonals confirm. Diagonals not congruent → not rectangle, not square. Rhombus.
Both pairs parallel → parallelogram. One right angle → rectangle. All sides equal → rhombus. Both → square.
Quadrilateral: 360° sum. Trapezoid: one pair parallel sides, midsegment formula. Isosceles trapezoid adds: congruent legs, congruent base angles, congruent diagonals.
A rectangle needs only four right angles. A square also needs four congruent sides.
Kite + trapezoid with parallel sides → all four sides must be congruent to satisfy both conditions. This is a rhombus.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Rhombus.
2) Square.
3) See list.
4) False.
5) Rhombus.

summary: The quadrilateral family tree organizes seven types in a nested hierarchy. Properties flow downward: a more specific type inherits all properties of its ancestors. A square inherits from rhombus, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, and quadrilateral. The hierarchy eliminates redundant proof and reveals the logical structure of quadrilateral geometry.

review_questions_image.alt: Complete family tree with all properties at each level.
review_questions_image.caption: Review of Quadrilateral Family Tree.
